Sessions and Tokens are two fundamentally different ways to handle user authentication, while Cookies and Local Storage are just the browser-side storage mechanisms used to hold them
Sessions vs Tokens
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This topic exists because HTTP is stateless. After a user logs in, the server has no memory of them on the next request.
Sessions/Tokens answers one question: how does the server re-establish “who is this” on every request without asking for the password again.
They differ in where the answer of ‘‘who is this?’’ lives (client/server).

Sessions (stateful)
After the user logins and the server successfully validates the credentials, the server:

• creates a session record in a store (Database / Caching system (Redis) / File in the filesystem).

• sends the client a random opaque ID.
The ID means nothing on its own; it is just a key. On each request the client sends the ID back, the server looks it up, finds the record, and knows who you are. So the server holds the identity / The client holds a pointer to it.
Consequences that follow directly from that:

• Revocation is trivial. Delete the record and the session is dead on the next request.

• Every request costs a lookup against the store.

• That (DB, Redis…) must be shared across all your servers, or you need sticky sessions (servers share same DB). which is bad for scaling.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Tokens (stateless, JWT)
After the user logins and the server successfully validates the credentials, the server:

• builds a token containing the identity claims (user ID, roles, expiry), then signs it.

• sends the client the token.
On each request the client sends the token back, and the server verifies the signature. If the signature is valid and not expired, the server trusts the claims inside, no need to any lookup on the DB, Cashing system..
this means that the client holds the identity. The server holds only the signing key. (Not like the session type).
Consequences:

• No DB, Redis querying per-request, This is the scaling win, you will only need the Secret.

• Revocation is the weak point. Once issued, a token is valid until it expires. You cannot easily kill it early without adding server-side state, which defeats the reason you went stateless.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Cookies vs Local Storage:
Both store data on the client. For auth the question is “where do you keep the credential proof of the user (session ID or token), and how does it get attached to requests?” It comes down to three things:
- who can read it.
- how it travels.
- what attack it exposes you to.
The defining difference: automatic vs manual
Cookies are sent automatically. Once set, the browser attaches the cookie to every request to that domain.
localStorage is manual. It is a key-value store the browser never touches on its own. Your JavaScript reads the value and explicitly puts it on each request, normally as Authorization: Bearer <value>.
Who can read it (the XSS attack)
localStorage is readable by any JavaScript on the page. If an attacker injects script (XSS), one line steals the credential.
Cookies can be hidden from JavaScript with the httpOnly flag. Such a cookie cannot be read by document.cookie or any JS. This is the main security reason to prefer cookies.
How it travels (the CSRF attack)
Cookies expose you to CSRF, i have already explain this in my previous BLOG.
localStorage is immune to CSRF. Nothing is sent automatically, so a forged cross-site request carries no credential.
The trade, stated plainly
You do not pick the safe option. You pick which attack you defend against:

• Cookie (httpOnly): safe from XSS token theft, exposed to CSRF. CSRF has clean, standard defenses.

• localStorage: safe from CSRF, exposed to XSS token theft. XSS theft has no clean defense once it happens; the token is gone.
Cookies win for browser apps because the attack they expose is well-solved, while the one localStorage exposes is not.
Smaller practical differences
Size they can hold:
- Cookies are capped (~4KB)
- localStorage holds more (~5MB)
Behaviour:
- Cookies have built-in expiry (Max-Age/Expires) and scoping (Domain, Path).
- localStorage cleanup is entirely your code's job.
The one thing to remember
This axis is only about where the credential lives. It is independent of whether the credential is a session ID or a JWT. In a browser, default to an httpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookie, and reach for localStorage only when you have a specific reason and have accepted the XSS exposure.
Wish you had fun reading, good luck !
